exercises.title: A: Word Formation
exercises.instructions: Use the word given in capitals to form a word that fits in the space.
exercises.studentInstruction: Use the word given in capitals to form a word that fits in the space.

items.sentence: A defining (1) ...... (CHARACTER) of the digital age is that individuals are constantly exposed to information from every corner of the globe.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun from CHARACTER, so the correct answer is "characteristic".

wrongOptions.1: The bare infinitive (1) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple misses the link to now — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect (have/has + past participle) links past actions to now or unfinished time.

items.sentence: Whereas previous generations could live (2) ...... (SEPARATE) from global events, widespread internet access now means that developments in distant countries feel anything but (3) ...... (FAMILIAR) to us.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is adverb + adjective, so the correct answer is "separately, unfamiliar".

wrongOptions.2: The bare infinitive (2) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals and semi-modals express ability, obligation, permission, or possibility — match the nuance in the sentence.

items.sentence: When news spreads without context, (4) ...... (RACE) and other forms of prejudice can quickly take hold online.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun from RACE, so the correct answer is "racism".

wrongOptions.4: The bare infinitive (4) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals and semi-modals express ability, obligation, permission, or possibility — match the nuance in the sentence.

items.sentence: Many users feel a deep (5) ...... (LOYAL) to particular platforms or online communities and struggle to develop an (6) ...... (APPRECIATE) of perspectives that differ from their own.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is lOYAL + APPRECIATE, so the correct answer is "loyalty, appreciation".

wrongOptions.5: The bare infinitive (5) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (5) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: There is growing concern that our digital (7) ...... (INHERIT) — the data we leave behind — is at risk, and that careless practices could lead to the (8) ...... (APPEAR) of privacy as we once knew it.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is iNHERIT + APPEAR, so the correct answer is "inheritance, disappearance".

wrongOptions.7: The bare infinitive (7) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals and semi-modals express ability, obligation, permission, or possibility — match the nuance in the sentence.

items.sentence: Whether such fears are grounded in evidence or are merely a matter of (9) ...... (PERCEIVE) remains debated, yet most experts agree that living in (10) ...... (RELATE) safety online requires education and awareness.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is pERCEIVE + RELATE, so the correct answer is "perception, relative".

wrongOptions.9: The bare infinitive (9) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple misses the link to now — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect (have/has + past participle) links past actions to now or unfinished time.

exercises.title: B: Gerund or Infinitive
exercises.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.
exercises.studentInstruction: Tap the correct word or phrase.

items.sentenceAfter: the project manager at the office first thing tomorrow.
items.originalSentence: We've agreed to **meet / meeting** the project manager at the office first thing tomorrow.
items.completedSentence: We've agreed to meet the project manager at the office first thing tomorrow.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "meet" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present simple describes habits, routines and general facts. Present continuous describes actions happening now or temporary situations.

wrongOptions.meeting: "meeting" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("the project manager at the office first thing tomorrow.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: Look for time clues — frequency words (very often, usually) point to present simple; right now and at the moment point to present continuous.

optionContrast.meeting: "meeting" doesn't fit here → "meet" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("the project manager at the office first thing tomorrow.").

items.sentenceAfter: your voice? I'm trying to concentrate.
items.originalSentence: Would you mind to **lower / lowering** your voice? I'm trying to concentrate.
items.completedSentence: Would you mind to lowering your voice? I'm trying to concentrate.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "lowering" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present simple describes habits, routines and general facts. Present continuous describes actions happening now or temporary situations.

wrongOptions.lower: "lower" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("your voice? I'm trying to concentrate.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: Look for time clues — frequency words (very often, usually) point to present simple; right now and at the moment point to present continuous.

optionContrast.lower: "lower" doesn't fit here → "lowering" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("your voice? I'm trying to concentrate.").

items.sentenceAfter: to a colleague in that manner.
items.originalSentence: No circumstance justifies to **speak / speaking** to a colleague in that manner.
items.completedSentence: No circumstance justifies to speaking to a colleague in that manner.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "speaking" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present simple describes habits, routines and general facts. Present continuous describes actions happening now or temporary situations.

wrongOptions.speak: "speak" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("to a colleague in that manner.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: Look for time clues — frequency words (very often, usually) point to present simple; right now and at the moment point to present continuous.

optionContrast.speak: "speak" doesn't fit here → "speaking" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("to a colleague in that manner.").

items.sentenceBefore: The coach was unhappy about the sponsor giving the team such expensive equipment, so they were made
items.originalSentence: The coach was unhappy about the sponsor giving the team such expensive equipment, so they were made **return / to return** it.
items.completedSentence: The coach was unhappy about the sponsor giving the team such expensive equipment, so they were made to return it.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "to return" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "to return" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "return" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.return: "return" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("it.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.return: "return" doesn't fit here → "to return" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("it.").

items.sentenceAfter: the sun rise over the mountains on our first morning there.
items.originalSentence: I'll never forget to **watch / watching** the sun rise over the mountains on our first morning there.
items.completedSentence: I'll never forget to watching the sun rise over the mountains on our first morning there.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "watching" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present simple describes habits, routines and general facts. Present continuous describes actions happening now or temporary situations.

wrongOptions.watch: "watch" does not fit a habit or general fact — the present simple is needed here.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Look for time clues — frequency words (very often, usually) point to present simple; right now and at the moment point to present continuous.

optionContrast.watch: "watch" doesn't fit here → "watching" does, because it does not fit a habit or general fact — the present simple is needed here.

items.sentenceAfter: out to the support team should you need any assistance.
items.originalSentence: Please don't hesitate to **reach / reaching** out to the support team should you need any assistance.
items.completedSentence: Please don't hesitate to reach out to the support team should you need any assistance.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "reach" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present simple describes habits, routines and general facts. Present continuous describes actions happening now or temporary situations.

wrongOptions.reaching: "reaching" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("out to the support team should you need any assistance.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: Look for time clues — frequency words (very often, usually) point to present simple; right now and at the moment point to present continuous.

optionContrast.reaching: "reaching" doesn't fit here → "reach" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("out to the support team should you need any assistance.").

items.sentenceBefore: At this rate, we're going to end up to
items.sentenceAfter: our reservation if we don't leave soon.
items.originalSentence: At this rate, we're going to end up to **lose / losing** our reservation if we don't leave soon.
items.completedSentence: At this rate, we're going to end up to losing our reservation if we don't leave soon.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "losing" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present simple describes habits, routines and general facts. Present continuous describes actions happening now or temporary situations.

wrongOptions.lose: "lose" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("our reservation if we don't leave soon.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: Look for time clues — frequency words (very often, usually) point to present simple; right now and at the moment point to present continuous.

optionContrast.lose: "lose" doesn't fit here → "losing" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("our reservation if we don't leave soon.").

items.sentenceAfter: up the subject of the cancelled holiday with my sister.
items.originalSentence: I didn't dare to **bring / bringing** up the subject of the cancelled holiday with my sister.
items.completedSentence: I didn't dare to bring up the subject of the cancelled holiday with my sister.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "bring" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present simple describes habits, routines and general facts. Present continuous describes actions happening now or temporary situations.

wrongOptions.bringing: "bringing" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("up the subject of the cancelled holiday with my sister.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: Look for time clues — frequency words (very often, usually) point to present simple; right now and at the moment point to present continuous.

optionContrast.bringing: "bringing" doesn't fit here → "bring" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("up the subject of the cancelled holiday with my sister.").

exercises.title: C: Key Word Transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.

items.sentence:
Elena and her grandmother are remarkably similar in character. **after**
Elena really ...... , doesn't she?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is resemble in character, so the correct answer is "takes after her grandmother".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "takes after her grandmother", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
I noticed I was sitting in the wrong lecture hall entirely. **myself**
I ...... in completely the wrong lecture hall.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is suddenly realised, so the correct answer is "found myself sitting".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "found myself sitting", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
The first question in the test was so simple that nobody expected anyone to get it wrong. **play**
The first question ...... , so nobody expected anyone to get it wrong.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is very easy, so the correct answer is "was child's play".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "was child's play", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
Daniel and his new flatmate have absolutely nothing they both enjoy. **common**
Daniel doesn't ...... his new flatmate.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is share interests, so the correct answer is "have anything in common with".

wrongOptions.anything in common with: This form misses the auxiliary — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "have anything in common with", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
Once she finishes her PhD, she'll be able to pursue her passion for painting. **free**
Completing her PhD ...... her passion for painting.
items.answer: will free her up to pursue
acceptedAnswers: will free her up to pursue

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is give time/freedom, so the correct answer is "will free her up to pursue".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "will free her up to pursue", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
My new neighbour and I got along brilliantly from the very first day. **house**
My new neighbour and I ...... from the very first day.
items.answer: got on like a house on fire
acceptedAnswers: got on like a house on fire

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is got on brilliantly, so the correct answer is "got on like a house on fire".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "got on like a house on fire", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
Are you in the mood for a walk along the seafront this afternoon? **like**
Do you ...... along the seafront this afternoon?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is want to do, so the correct answer is "feel like walking".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "feel like walking", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
Tom resigned because he and the head of department had very different views. **eye**
Tom resigned because he ...... the head of department.
items.answer: didn't see eye to eye with
acceptedAnswers: didn't see eye to eye with

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is disagree, so the correct answer is "didn't see eye to eye with".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "didn't see eye to eye with", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

exercises.title: D: Prepositions and Fixed Phrases
exercises.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: Sofia was fortunate to be born .................... a family with a long tradition of academic achievement.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is born into, so the correct answer is "into".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: The old lighthouse features .................... a short film about the region's maritime history.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is features in a film, so the correct answer is "in".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Many economists argue that the minimum wage should be equal .................... at least sixty percent of the median salary.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is equal to, so the correct answer is "to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence: The proposed regulation is problematic .................... one key respect: it fails to specify who is responsible for enforcement.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is in one respect, so the correct answer is "in".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence: The committee decided it would not be .................... the public interest to release the full report at this stage.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is public interest, so the correct answer is "in".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: This particular variety of orchid is native .................... the rainforests of Borneo.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is native to, so the correct answer is "to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: The director was unable to attend the awards ceremony .................... person, so a colleague accepted the prize on her behalf.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is in person, so the correct answer is "in".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Sailing across the bay at dawn is, for many visitors, the nearest thing .................... a truly peaceful experience.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is nearest thing to, so the correct answer is "to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.
